appendices.title: How and Why Davening Works and What It Accomplishes

segments.enText: APPENDIX 1
HOW AND WHY DAVENING WORKS AND WHAT IT ACCOMPLISHES
Introduction

We must first explain some crucial *hashkafah* concepts in order to answer these questions. (Sorry, some questions do not have short answers! And no, you cannot skip this introduction and go straight to the answers! And—you must do the questions in order!)

Rav Dessler (*Michtav Me'Eliyahu, Kuntres Ha'bechirah, chelek* 4) introduces us to the fundamental concept of *mazal elyon* and *mazal tachton*, by first giving us a deep understanding of the term "*mazal*."

Rav Dessler explains that *mazal*, which comes from the word "*nozel*" (liquid or flow), refers to all the stuff that Hashem "pours down" on you from birth (e.g., where you were born, when you were born, who your parents are, your talents and handicaps—both physical and emotional) and all the stuff that happens in life (e.g., the people He arranges you to meet, winning lotteries, *chas v'shalom* getting cancer, etc.). All these things are the tools He gives you so you can fulfill your many particular jobs on this earth. Perhaps your job is to be that poor person who makes the correct choice to be happy with what he has and be an inspiration to others (rather than making the incorrect choice of being cranky and angry). In addition, poor people provide others with the opportunity to do the mitzvah of tzedakah. Someone needs to be poor; without poor people, how would everyone else be challenged between an evening watching dumb videos or volunteering for Tomchei Shabbos? Needing Tomchei Shabbos packages is an important job—and Hashem chose you!

Of course, someone also needs to be rich so they'll have to deal with deciding whether to waste five thousand dollars on a watch no one will ever see or to give that amount to Tomchei Shabbos. That's also an important job! Same goes with being sick or healthy, having stable parents or growing up in a dysfunctional home, or being the kid who tutors others or the one who gets tutored! Someone needs to be the person who inspires everyone (or not!) by losing their job and downsizing their house while smiling and saying, "*Gam zu l'tovah!*—It's all for the best!" Similarly, someone needs to suddenly make a fortune and remain humble and keep his same, simple lifestyle (or not!). You get the idea.

FAQ: But some people seem to have a really "fun" *mazal*—brains, money, a happy home, good looks, and/or an easygoing nature—while others seem to have a really lousy "not fun" *mazal*—learning disabilities, messed-up home, physical disabilities, and/or an awkward personality. That seems not fair!
It's a famous question—and a good one! *See Appendix 2 below, but first continue reading!*
Rav Dessler asks: Can one daven to change their *mazal*? How would that sound?

"Hashem, I think I've been doing a really good job with the *mazal* you gave me. I'm here in the hospital, I smile, I say '*gam zu l'tovah*,' I cheer up all the other patients, I'm nice to all the people who are using me as their *chessed* case and are really nudniks, but I pretend to be happy to see them. How about switching things up so that I become that person who miraculously recovers and still doesn't take things for granted, and inspires others, etc.? That's also an important job; can I now have that one?"

Rav Dessler quotes what seem to be contradictory answers from Chazal (you can see all those quotes if you look up *Kuntres Ha'bechirah, chelek* 4) and resolves the seeming contradiction by explaining that there are two kinds of *mazal*:

One is *mazal elyon*. That's all the stuff you were given as part of your "jobs" and challenges on earth. Hopefully, you'll make the correct decisions, do the best that Hashem knows you can under the circumstances, do whatever *tikkun* is needed, increase *kedushah* in the world, and earn *Olam Haba* for that! Or you'll mess up, not do what Hashem knows you could have done, decrease *kedushah*, and earn Gehinnom. It's your choice; you have free will. The stuff that you were born with, or that happens along the way in life, cannot be changed—they're what you're out here for; they are your jobs! Deal with it. You get what you get and you say, "Thank you"! That's *mazal elyon*. ("But my *mazal elyon* really stinks compared to that other kid's!" Relax; we'll deal with that later.)

But a lot of stuff is not part of your main job but is rather stuff that exists to make your main jobs easier (or harder) to handle. Being sick in the hospital is a difficult "job," but it's easier if you have money. Being poor is a difficult "job," but it's easier if you are healthy. That secondary, "not main job" kind of stuff is *mazal tachton* and is changeable! Hashem starts you off with your *mazal elyon* jobs, and based on how you're doing, He can make the *mazal tachton* stuff easier or worse! How do we get Hashem to make our *mazal tachton* stuff better? Well, certainly by doing our *mazal elyon* job well, doing mitzvos, and, most importantly, by talking to Him! Every time you turn to Hashem and show Him (and yourself) that you realize He is in total control, it raises your spiritual level, your *neshamah* gets a bit more pure, you are a different person than yesterday, and that makes you possibly deserve a better *mazal tachton*.

"Well, duh—I know that Hashem is in control, and He knows I know that, so what do I have to say it for?!" Not everyone realizes, but there's a big difference between "I have it in my heart" as contrasted to verbalizing it—getting it out in the open—and actually talking directly to Hashem and creating a relationship.

So, if you're asking for changes in stuff that's your *mazal elyon*, then obviously Hashem must say no. That's your job! On the other hand, if what you're asking for relates to *mazal tachton* stuff, then possibly, if you deserve it, Hashem could say yes to your request.
"But how are we supposed to know what stuff in our lives is *mazal elyon* and what is *mazal tachton*?" We don't!

"Rabbi Feigenbaum, that's not funny! How do we know what we can daven for and what we can't?" Well, Rav Dessler brings a story from the Gemara about Rav Elazar ben Pedas who constantly davened to get out of his poverty. Hashem spoke to him and told him that it simply won't happen since poverty is his *mazal elyon*. But assuming you're not on the level of Rav Pedas—meaning that Hashem doesn't talk to you directly—then you don't know!

"So what do I do?" You daven! You show Hashem that you accept that He is ultimately in control, you create a relationship with Him, you thank Him for what you do have, and thus you raise your own spiritual level and your own *kedushah*. If Hashem says yes to your requests, great! If He chooses not to, also great!

"But if He decides to turn down my requests since they refer to my *mazal elyon*, what happens to all those *tefillos, kabbalos,* or extra *chessed* I did to deserve a 'yes' answer? They don't go to waste! You earned reward, and this will perhaps go for some *mazal tachton* areas or for *Olam Haba* (that's not a bad thing, you know!)."
Summarize

How does *tefillah* work? By turning to Hashem and showing Him that you appreciate what He has given you, and you accept that He is in control, you bring yourself closer to Him and raise your spiritual level. The same is true when you notch up the quality of your *avodas Hashem*. Today you are a very different "you" than yesterday—more spiritual and with a better balance sheet of mitzvos versus *aveiros*. And thus perhaps the "you" of today is deserving of a different *mazal tachton*.

FAQ: But how does it work if I daven for someone else, especially someone so sick they cannot even daven for themselves? He or she is not doing any extra mitzvah that would make them worthy of a different *mazal*.

Great question. Let's answer with a *mashal*: Your friend, who is a bit selfish, wants to stay home Thursday night and just do nothing. You convince him to come along with you and do some *chessed*. He gets the mitzvah of *chessed* and gets rewarded for that. His *neshamah* is now a bit more *kadosh*. What about you? Besides the *chessed* that you also did, you get an additional reward for getting someone else to do a mitzvah! Every time you cause someone to do a mitzvah (or stop them from doing something inappropriate), you get reward. That itself raises your *neshamah*! So the person you are davening for also got people to do mitzvos. How did he do that? By being sick! Because of him, you said an extra *tefillah* or gave a little extra *tzedakah*. And that sick person gets rewarded for that, which then raises his *neshamah*. And if his sickness is his *mazal tachton*, that may just push him over the threshold and he will deserve to get better! (Of course, if it's his *mazal elyon*, then he still gets the reward, but it will be used for something else, like we said above.)
Now Let's Get to the FAQs
*שֶׁעָשָׂה לִי כֹּל צְרְכִּי*—If that's true, then why should we ask Hashem for stuff? He already gave me all I need!

Yes, He did give you all you need! And one of the things you "need" is to create a relationship with Hashem and talk to Him on a regular basis! What was the curse of the snake? "*V'afar tochal kol yemei chayecha*—Dust you will eat all the days of your life." The snake eats dust. Why is that a curse? Hashem is giving him all his food forever, everywhere, and at any time! The answer is that this itself is the curse! The snake never needs to turn to Hashem; Hashem wants nothing to do with him. "Here, take your stuff; go away and don't bother Me ever again!" That is the greatest curse.

"But if Hashem would just give me everything I wanted right away, I'd still talk to Him regularly to thank Him for my great life!" Oh, sure! Be honest! That is very unlikely. We very quickly take the good for granted. And Hashem knows that too. Therefore, Hashem creates situations that push us to talk to Him in order to help us create a relationship with Him.
FAQ: Why am I asking Hashem for stuff? Hashem knows what I want, and He also knows what I really need! If I really need it and am supposed to have it, He would have given it to me!
FAQ: Hashem knows I am sick and knows I obviously want to get better! If He wanted me healed, He would have healed me without my asking!

Nope! Perhaps He wants you to stretch yourself spiritually! Hashem started you with a certain *mazal tachton*, and now you need to raise your spiritual level through *tefillah* to deserve that change! As we said above, some things that you need to help make your *mazal elyon* job easier you must earn on your own! Thus you may need to be healed (and Hashem knows that), but you will only get it if you deserve it. And as we said, the best way to earn it is by raising your spiritual level through actually taking the time to verbalize what you want and having a sincere and heartfelt discussion with Hashem.
FAQ: Why should I bother making requests from Hashem? I've asked Him many times before for stuff, and He keeps on saying no!
FAQ: Hashem "heals the sick"? No, He doesn't! I've asked to be healed many times, and He still says no!

Right! And there are many reasons that Hashem may choose to turn down your request. As humans, it's a little silly to think that we can understand all of Hashem's calculations! (See Appendix 2 and a super *mashal* of this.) Perhaps He said no because you weren't sincere or worthy enough, so you must try harder. Perhaps you were truly sincere, but if you could see a video of what you were like on this earth three hundred years ago, you'd understand very well why all this is happening! We do believe in the concept of *gilgul*, reincarnation. The *Ramchal* in *Derech Hashem* (the chapter "*Hashgachah Ishis*—Individual Providence") goes on at length about the fact that our actions in earlier *gilgulim* could have an affect on the *mazal* of our present *gilgul*. And since you and I have no way of knowing that information, there will be times when we simply cannot figure out why Hashem is saying no to our requests. Perhaps, somehow the experience of this struggle is important for you in the future. When the time comes, you'll look back and say, "Aha! Now I get it"! (And then you can print it in a book of *hashgachah pratis* stories or be part of some inspiring video!) And of course, as we said above, perhaps what you're requesting is simply your *mazal elyon* and therefore unchangeable. Since you never know, you keep asking (hopefully, with a smile on your face and without frustration), and that raises your *neshamah* no matter what, earns you reward, adds *kedushah* to the world, and brings Mashiach a bit closer. And that's not a bad thing!
"But Rabbi Feigenbaum, my life, and the whole world, just looks so haphazard!"
True—next question!
APPENDIX 2
TZADDIK V'RA LO
FAQ: Thank you? For what? My life stinks!
FAQ: What? It certainly doesn't look like Hashem "provides all of us with all we need"! There are people out there really suffering!
FAQ: But how am I supposed to now sing all these praises to Hashem when my life is so difficult? I have so much stuff going on, and it's hard to accept that all is fair and just!

FAQ: What? It certainly doesn't look like Hashem "does good to everyone"! There are people out there really suffering! And my life is a mess! I certainly don't see "prosperity, success, and relief from our troubles"!
FAQ: "Never did I see a *tzaddik* who was forsaken (by Hashem), whose children had to go begging for food." But that's not what it looks like!

This is the famous question of *"tzaddik v'ra lo"*—why some (seemingly) good people have such lousy lives. And it does appear to us that Hashem has not divided up the *mazal elyon* very fairly among people, as we asked above. To be honest, this is a very complex topic, which is really for another time and another book—*im yirtzeh Hashem*. This is not the place for a lengthy discussion as to how Hashem makes His decisions, neither is this the place to prove to you that clearly Hashem has a plan that makes sense and that He runs the world with careful forethought and planning (*"HaTzur tamim pa'alo ki chol derachav mishpat*—All that Hashem does is perfect, for all His paths are just"). All that is for another time.

But I know that this topic is very painful, and for some people (teens and adults) it makes davening and turning to Hashem very difficult. So I will try to at least answer the following question: "I accept that Hashem has an intelligent plan for all that happens. But I am not stupid! If there is such an intelligent plan, how come I can't see it? How come to us it seems to make no sense, if it supposedly really does make sense?"
The answer to that is the following *mashal*. It's not mine! I heard it sourced to Rav Elchonon Wasserman and Rav Yitzchok Hutner. It's long, but read it carefully, as it explains a lot.

Imagine a person who grew up in the city and has no idea where food comes from. He just goes to the supermarket and sees bread on the shelves every day and buys a loaf without thinking at all how bread is manufactured or what ingredients are used to create it. It's just there like everything else in the store!

One day, he takes a drive out to the countryside. He sees a nice empty plot of land and thinks to himself that this plot of land is nice and smooth and could be used for building a home. All of a sudden, he sees some person dragging some kind of huge knife up and down the land—completely messing it up! Now it's ugly mud! And even crazier, this person is dropping little "things" into this mud. He goes over to check it out, picks up one of those little things and realizes they are edible. He thinks to himself, "What a bad person! He turns a wonderful piece of land into mud and then throws yummy seeds into it!" This guy must be evil or crazy! Our city slicker decides to wait and see what happens. After a while (use your imagination!), he sees pretty, brownish "flowers" growing out of the ground. Now he changes his mind and says to himself, "*Oy*! I was all wrong. This person is not some evil guy who throws food into the mud. He's a good person who grows pretty flowers."

Just as he is about to leave, he sees the man cutting down those flowers. "Oh, he's probably going to give them to someone," thinks our city person. But then he sees that man grind those pretty flowers into a powder! "This guy is an evil maniac!" says our city person to himself. "First I thought he was a bad person for messing up a nice field. Then I thought he was even worse for throwing away yummy seeds. Then I thought I misjudged him and that, really, he was a good person since he was growing pretty flowers to give to someone. Now I realize that he is more crazy and evil than I thought in the beginning! He grows flowers and then wantonly destroys them! That is nuts! I better get out of here!"
Just as he is about to leave, he sees the man pouring water and other stuff into that powder. He goes over to check it out. He sticks his finger in and tastes it—delicious!

"What is that," he asks the man, "and what are you?" The man tells him that he is a farmer, and that stuff is called dough. "Amazing, this stuff is great," says the man. And now he is ready to leave, and he thinks to himself the following: "What a fool I was! I never understood anything! What I thought was bad—destroying the land, grinding the flowers—was not bad. And what I thought was good—growing pretty flowers—was not good! It was all part of a process to make this stuff called dough. Now I understand everything! It all now makes sense."

As he gets into his car, he sees the farmer take that yummy dough, roll it into a ball, and throw it into a fire! "What?! That is completely crazy, random, evil, and senseless! Now absolutely nothing at all that I have seen makes any sense at all. Why would anyone work that hard on something so wonderful and throw it into a fire? That farmer guy must be a maniac."

And then he sees a loaf of bread come out of the oven! Now he realizes that he actually never understood anything. What he thought was good was not really good. And what he thought was bad was not really bad. Of course, since he never knew where bread comes from and never knew the process, he had no way of imagining how grinding "pretty flowers" could have any relationship to the bread he buys in the supermarket. And had he not stayed until the end, he would have been positive he understood everything, when, of course, he hadn't!

That is us in this world. As humans, we don't know the "process," and we certainly can't see the end product! That will only happen when Mashiach comes. What we think now is the "good" may not be, and certainly what appears to be the "bad" may not be bad. We are just like that city person. We see just part of something; we don't know how the process works, and we can't see the end of six thousand years of history. We do know that Hashem told us that all is just. We have also seen the miraculous survival of Bnei Yisrael. Therefore, when we encounter something we don't understand, we say, "*Gam zu l'tovah*—It is all for the best."

appendices.title: The Positive Power of Making Mistakes

segments.enText: THE POSITIVE POWER OF MAKING MISTAKES
FAQ: But I can't "stand up straight"! I've tried, but I keep falling back down! All the stuff in my life is crushing me!
FAQ: But I keep on messing up! Maybe I should just give up. I've tried so many times to do the right thing, and I keep on failing!
FAQ: Fresh start? I've tried so many times, and it doesn't work! Why shouldn't I just give up?
We start our day by saying *Modeh Ani*. In the siddur, we explained it in the following way:

*Thank You, Hashem, for giving my neshamah back to me* (which was a merciful thing to do, since I have not yet lived up to my full potential, and therefore may not really deserve that); *Your belief in me is very great!* (You believe I am important; my struggles are important; and You believe I can climb up to the next rung—that is why You woke me up. Thank You!)

The very fact that Hashem woke us up shows that He believes in each and every one of us! He does believe that you can push forward, and He does believe that you can climb to the next level in your *avodas Hashem*! You just need to believe in yourself as much as Hashem believes in you! (Please read that again!)
"But I keep on failing!" "I keep on making mistakes!" Right, me too. So does everyone!

That is the process of growth. The *pasuk* (*Mishlei 24:16*) says clearly, "*Sheva yipol tzaddik v'kam*—The *tzaddik* falls down seven times and gets back up." *Tzaddikim* fall. What makes someone a *tzaddik* is not that they don't fall, but rather that they get up!

Rav Hutner takes it further. He explains that making mistakes is an integral part of the growth process! Rav Hutner says that the true meaning of the *pasuk* "*Sheva yipol tzaddik v'kam*" is that the only way to become a *tzaddik* is through the process of making mistakes! It is not simply that *tzaddikim* are human and thus make mistakes. The only way they become *tzaddikim* is by exercising their *bechirah* (free choice), and perforce sometimes making mistakes along the way until they figure it all out! The very struggle with our *yetzer hara*, the stumblings, tactical retreats, crashes, and self-disappointment all are a necessary, normal, and healthy part of the growth process!

Listen to one of my very favorite *vorts*: We know that the thirty-nine prohibited *melachos* (creative work) of Shabbos come from the Mishkan, the traveling Tabernacle. Whatever was done to create a Mishkan we do not do on Shabbos since we do not create on Shabbos. One of the *melachos* is *mochek*, erasing. Where was erasing used in creating the Mishkan? When they made the *kerashim*, the boards, they needed to write symbols on them so that when they were reassembled at each stop, they would know where each board went (each board had to be in the exact same position each time they erected the Mishkan). What if you wrote the wrong symbol? You had to erase it, and thus the prohibition of erasing on Shabbos. But why should that be considered a *meleches machsheves*, creative work? You only erased because you made a mistake! The answer is that you cannot build a Mishkan without making mistakes! Making mistakes is part of our growth process. As we said, a flat smooth straight line on your EKG means you are dead.
So assuming you woke up, that means that Hashem believes you have a crucial role to play and can conquer whatever He set in your way. If you fail, get back up; it means you are a *tzaddik*!

appendices.title: Am Segulah/Am Nivchar — the Chosen People

segments.enText: AM SEGULAH/AM NIVCHAR—THE CHOSEN PEOPLE
FAQ: But I do NOT understand the Torah, and to be honest, I am NOT happy that I was stuck being part of the chosen people! What's so great about it?
FAQ: Chosen people? Doesn't this *brachah* sound racist? I mean, I know a lot of really nice non-Jews—and some "not-so-great" Jews! Is there really such a difference?

Simply put, the fact that Hashem took us out of Egypt, and three million people all heard Him speak at Har Sinai—choosing us as His nation and giving us His Torah and a mission to the world—that is a historical reality! And just like you had no choice as to your complexion, height, or parents, you had no choice whether to be born as part of Klal Yisrael or not. You get what you get—deal with it!

Keep in mind that although this mission does come with a lot of responsibility and work, it is a beautiful way of life (full of depth, beauty, and meaning), and the Torah promises great reward. At my stage in life, since I have experienced more than you (and studied more Torah sources), I can see that! But it is possible that for many readers, at this moment there are more parts of Torah that don't make sense to you than those that do make sense! That does make it hard. And perhaps, unfortunately, your personal experience of Yiddishkeit has not been sweet for all sorts of reasons. That definitely makes it hard. But *Matan Torah* and us being Hashem's special treasure is a reality. (Proving that is not hard at all, but that topic is not for here but for another time and another book. But there are resources out there that cover this topic fully, and a simple look at the miraculous survival of Bnei Yisrael is proof itself!). So if you were born into it, then you need to get with the program, do your best, and hang on until you learn, experience more, and access the sweetness, which I assure you is there!

What does it mean to be Hashem's *am segulah*, His special treasure? Obviously, it doesn't mean that we are automatically nicer and better people than everyone else. Unfortunately, we all see that this is not true! Rather, it means that Hashem has given us an extra bit of "Godliness," an extra *kedushah* and a special *neshamah* that has the potential to allow us to get closer to Hashem than someone who does not have this *segulah*. But it is only potential. It's up to you whether you get in touch with that extra *kedushah* and develop it or not.

Who has this special *neshamah* with this *segulah*? Anyone born from a Jewish mother is born with it and thus automatically has the responsibilities that come with that, as we said above. As well, anyone is welcome to join the chosen people and can also get that special *neshamah* and that *segulah*! When non-Jews convert, they get that special *neshamah*, which is why the Gemara views *geirim* as having been born anew. But they must go through the process and need to agree to keep all the responsibilities (613 mitzvos) that come with that special *neshamah* and unique mission to the world.
Appendix 5
Bechirah Chofshis — Free Will
FAQ
Which “steps” does Hashem decide and set for us, and which do we choose on our own?
FAQ
If in the end “only the plan of Hashem will prevail,” no matter what humans think and plan, where does our free will fit in?

We can’t fully explain here the sources, details, and complexity of our free will and Hashem’s *hashgachah* and providence. There are entire books on the subject—and this is just an appendix to a siddur! Still, let us give the very basis in a nutshell.

As the *Rambam* (Chapter 8 of *Shemoneh Perakim*) explains: “*Ha’kol b’yedei Shamayim chutz mi’yiras Shamayim*—Everything is controlled by Hashem except for decisions that relate to our fear of Heaven,” i.e., mitzvah or *aveirah* choices. Meaning, He controls our reality and sets up our situations. He decided that I will face a choice of whether to give a hundred dollars or two hundred dollars to *tzedakah*, and not the choice of whether to give one million or two million dollars to *tzedakah* (since He did not give me that much money). But He will not make my choice of which of those two amounts to give or if I will give any *tzedakah* at all.

Sometimes, He will control our “minor” decisions that have no moral weight of right or wrong. There are two different streets you can take to get home. Taking Street A or Street B is not a right/wrong, good/bad moral decision. Hashem may thus intervene in your decision process because He needs you on Street A. But whether you speed, whether you pick up the person on the corner who needs a ride, and what music you listen to as you drive—all those are right/wrong, good/bad *yiras Shamayim* moral decisions, and those you decide.
(“But the reality He gave me, and therefore the choices I face, are harder and less fun than the reality and choices of my friend! That’s not fair!” Good point! See Appendix 2.)

Hopefully, you will make the correct decisions, and by doing that you will help move Hashem’s master plan of *“l’sakein olam b’malchus Shakkai,”* that the entire world will accept Hashem’s dominion, forward. This will hasten the arrival of Mashiach (and earn you reward!). But unfortunately, we don’t always make the correct decisions (as we see that Mashiach has not yet arrived!), and then Hashem has to readjust how things play out. Perhaps your *neshamah* will have to come back as a *gilgul* to fix things up. But in the end, taking all six thousand years into account (and do remember that the true reward for our good deeds is in *Olam Haba* anyway), it will all work out, and *“atzas Hashem hi sakum,”* Hashem’s plan will prevail. (See the amazing *mashal* in Appendix 2 that relates to this.)

I want to stress that this is a very simplistic overview of a very complex topic. If this is something you really want to understand, there are many good books that discuss it from all different perspectives.

appendices.title: Siyata D'Shmaya — Asking for Divine Assistance

segments.enText: Siyata D'Shmaya — Asking for Divine Assistance

FAQ: We say, "Do not allow the *yetzer hara* to get the better of us" and, "Compel our creative powers to be used to serve You." But I thought we had free will? This sounds like we are asking Hashem to eliminate our free will choices!

As we said above (in the introduction to Appendix 1) regarding *mazal elyon* and *mazal tachton* (if you skipped that, go back; otherwise, the following will make no sense), Hashem can create situations in our lives (if we are deserving) where it is easier for us to be successful in fulfilling our *mazal elyon* mission. He can also make it harder! (See also Appendix 5 regarding Hashem creating our life situations.)

By this request we are showing Hashem that we honestly do want to fulfill our mission, and are asking that He set up the reality of our day such that it will be relatively easy for us to decide to do the correct thing and that we will not be in situations where we will be tempted to make bad decisions. But in the end, any good/bad or right/wrong decision we make is completely up to our free will; see Appendix 5.
All That Hashem Does Is Good
FAQ
“Do for us good acts of kindness.” What does that mean? Are there “bad” acts of kindness?

All that Hashem does for us is good for us. But there are sweet, tasty medicines and bitter medicines! They are both good for us, but we certainly would prefer the sweet one! Similarly, we ask Hashem that the acts of kindness He does for us should be sweet and pleasant, rather than harsh and bitter. Similarly, on Rosh Hashanah, we wish everyone, “*Shanah tovah u'mesukah*—A good and sweet year.” Why “sweet”? Because of course it will be a good year; all that Hashem does is good! But we wish our friends that the good Hashem does for them will be a “sweet good.”
Appendix 8: Our Material World
FAQ: Well, to be honest, I just want all this stuff because...I want it! Is that so bad?
FAQ: Honestly, I don't really care that much about my unique mission in this world. I just daven because I need stuff from Hashem. Is that so bad?

In the fifth chapter of *Shemoneh Perakim*, the *Rambam* describes the ideal approach a person should take to running their lives, based on the Mishnah: "*V'chol maasecha yiheyu l'shem Shamayim*—Everything you do or say, everything we buy, and how we spend every minute of the day should all be based on what will maximize our *avodas Hashem*. In theory, why would we want something that will not help us in serving Hashem and help us in fulfilling our mission? What purpose would it serve? But the *Rambam* himself says that this is a very high level of running one's life, which is reached only by a select few and only after many years of work. But it is important to understand the concept and to have something to aim for.

We should put thought into why we want stuff and why we spend money on things. Obviously, we can't maximize our service of Hashem if we're tired, hungry, cranky, tense, and irritable. So we do need some stuff to solve those issues—each in our own way and on our own level. But even for "normal" people, acquiring things just out of jealousy or to make others jealous of us is not healthy. And certainly, to use our material things to create a false sense of self is not going to help us grow.

So to sum up, it is understandable that at a certain point in our lives we just "want stuff because I want it," and no, that's not so bad. But at least during our *tefillah* to Hashem we should have a sense of what we are aspiring to.
Appendix 9
Korbanos

FAQ: Really? Am I supposed to wake up each morning (and on Rosh Chodesh and Yom Tov) and say to myself: "I sooo wish I could go bring an animal sacrifice this morning"? How are we supposed to relate to a concept that seems to be so foreign to us?

First, let's get rid of some misconceptions. The word *"korban"* does not translate as "sacrifice" (as in giving something up), even though in English we seem to use the term "animal sacrifices." It also does not translate as "offering," as in bringing a gift or bribe to the angry gods, as it is in the way we view *avodah zarah*.

Rather, *korban* means "to come near." *Korbanos* help a person get closer to Hashem. When we had a Beis Hamikdash, we took the animal parts, and by placing them on the *Mizbei'ach* (Altar), they became elevated. This is a personal declaration of our intentions to elevate all the material stuff that Hashem gave us (our physical powers, emotional skills, possessions—whatever Hashem gave us) to a higher level, to direct it all toward serving Hashem and accomplishing our mission here on earth. In addition, the blood of the animal sprinkled on the *Mizbei'ach* serves as a reminder of our vulnerability. This "near-death" type of experience is meant to motivate us to continue growing spiritually and getting as close to Hashem as humanly possible while we're still on this earth—which is not forever!

To emphasize the point that *korbanos* are to bring us closer to Hashem rather than to "appease an angry god," it is interesting to note that the name of Hashem used in connection with *korbanos* is specifically His four-letter name that we do not pronounce, which is His attribute of love and mercy. The name Elokim (which represents His trait of strict judgment) only appears five times in the whole of *Sefer Vayikra*, which primarily discusses the *korbanos*, and always in the context of other nations, not Bnei Yisrael!

The above is a simple overview of the concept of *korbanos*. In truth, it is much more complex than that. Really, we would need to look at each detail of each different *korban*, since each *korban* was brought for different things. The choice of animal and details of the *avodah* reflected the concept and essence of that particular *korban* and symbolized to the person bringing the *korban* specifically what he should be focusing on! When we daven and ask Hashem to bring back the *korbanos*, really what we are saying is the following:

*Hashem, I know that there are very deep meanings in each and every korban, and that they serve to help me cleanse my sins and elevate my life. But in this galus we are so insensitive to kedushah that I simply cannot understand that! Please rebuild the Beis Hamikdash, give us back the ability to "smell" the beautiful aroma of kedushah and understand the power and meaning of the korbanos. And I am sure that when that happens, we will bring all the korbanos with joy!*

One last but very important point. All mitzvos have an inherent, underlying, and deep-seated reality and *kedushah* in their own right. And that goes way beyond any symbolism or explanation that we may give. Here we have mentioned some of the most common, basic "explanations" of *korbanos*. But in the end, if Hashem said that something is crucial for our *neshamos*, then it is! He created our *neshamos* and knows what is best for them, even if we have a hard time understanding that!
Appendix 10
Making Hashem "Happy"
FAQ: What does it mean that Hashem is "הבוחר בשירי זמרה"—takes pleasure in songs of praise"? Hashem is not human; what does it mean that He "takes pleasure"?

We always use "human terminology" when talking about Hashem (e.g., the "Hand of Hashem"), since we are human and cannot relate to Hashem in any other way. In this particular instance, the meaning is the following: Hashem creates challenges for us to overcome so that we can accomplish our mission and earn our reward. He wants us to pass those tests by overcoming those challenges! One of the biggest challenges we have in life is praising Hashem under all circumstances, even when we are dealing with things that are hard for us to understand. That's a tough challenge! When we do praise Hashem no matter what, we pass that test. Thus Hashem "takes pleasure," meaning He is happy with us when we do praise Him even when it's hard for us, since that shows that we have succeeded in dealing with that difficult challenge.
APPENDIX 11
BEN TORAH/BAS YISRAEL
FAQ: Is it so bad to be a woman that men thank Hashem that "You did not create me a woman"? And were men NOT created "according to Your will"?
FAQ: Women are also "committed to putting Torah into practice," so how come they don't put on tallis and tefillin?
FAQ: The general custom is that women do not say *Tachanun*. But why? Why the differences in *tefillah* (and practices) between men and women?
Introduction

I think everyone understands that this is not the place for a full discussion on this topic. Entire books have been written on this topic, whereas this is simply an appendix to a siddur! More importantly, a lot depends on your personal experience and your family background. Of all the FAQs here, it is this one that is most likely to be understood differently by different people—just as things look different to two people, one of whom is wearing green-tinted glasses and the other yellow-tinted glasses!

In addition, a lot depends on what you've already heard about this topic. Assuming you've had a good, standard yeshiva/Bais Yaakov education, you'll have heard all the many varied and different explanations taught to boys and girls. If they resonated with you and you were satisfied with the answers, then great! You don't need me. And if it all still bothers you, then one paragraph (even written by me!) may not help!

"Rabbi Feigenbaum, are you trying to just duck and avoid this? Is this going to be another one of those classic, 'Oh, it's very deep and we're not worthy of understanding…' non-answer answers? Are you going to hide behind a lot of words in the hope that we don't catch on that you have no answer? That is not why I bought this siddur!"
Absolutely not! I don't do that. But I am serious about what I said above that this particular topic is more suited for a real and complex discussion, and not for an appendix to a siddur.

"So why did you include the question?" To show you that it is a good question! Chazal were careful how they worded things, so if they worded two *brachos* the way they did, there was a reason or many reasons, and the topic deserves study. And most certainly there are reasons why certain mitzvos and practices are different for men and women.

"So you're not going to say anything on this topic?" I'm going to present the approach that resonates with me. Of all the ideas and answers I have read and heard, this one works for me. But I am me, and you are not. And I don't know what glasses you are wearing. I know what I'm writing, but I don't know what you're reading! If this helps, great! If not, also great! As I explained above, there are many approaches, all true on different levels, and you need the one that fits who you are.
I know that was really a long introduction to the following short answer! But in this case, the introduction was more important than the answer itself.
Now Let's Get to the FAQs

Here we go: We are all Hashem's children. As children, all are beloved in the eyes of Hashem. But to accomplish our great mission in the world (until Mashiach comes), each "kid" is given a different job. Hashem needs a lot of different positions filled. We need kings, judges, Kohanim, and Leviim. We had twelve Shevatim—each had its own job, own flag, and own colored stone on the *Choshen*. And we need men and women. In general, men connect to *kedushah* better when that connection is structured and involves doing things within specific guidelines. That structure and defined "doing" of mitzvos, at specific times with specific objects, helps focus on the spiritual and not get sidetracked from their mission. Women have a more intuitive, inner sense of *kedushah*, which in a certain way is more in line with Hashem's Will (intuitive, flowing *avodas Hashem*), since that's really how things should have been. Women, therefore, don't need all that structure and all those time constraints. These could actually hamper them by allowing the external aspects of the mitzvah to give them a false sense of *kedushah*, which is a risk men run all the time.

So if we compare those two ways of connecting to *kedushah* (men—defined and structured, as compared to women—intuitive and flowing), we discover the following: Practically, in this very material world, it is easier to have defined times and defined mitzvos and practices to force us to stop and connect to *kedushah*. Not having that is a harder challenge. Thus, when looking at it practically, men are happy that they were not given that more difficult challenge, and thus they say "*she'lo asani ishah*." Women, however, cannot say that they are happy they are not men, because that would make it sound like they are happy they have less mitzvos and are looking to get out of serving Hashem—which is completely not true!

In truth, women were made closer in line with God's Will (albeit, with a role that involves more difficult challenges), since intuitive *avodas Hashem* is in many areas actually the ideal. This is what women are thanking Hashem for when they say "*she'asani kirtzono*." They therefore also have less specific and defined mitzvos and practices. But as far as closeness to Hashem, everyone is judged as to how well they did their job! You may prefer some other job, and maybe at different times different jobs seem more glamorous or more prestigious. But as we said at the beginning of this answer, we are all Hashem's children. As children, all are beloved in the eyes of Hashem. And you will be judged on your mission and on how well you did your job—not how well you did someone else's!

I hope that made sense and helped. If yes, great. If that made things worse, I apologize! As I wrote earlier: If these answers raise even more questions, great! Go find books on the subject, ask your *rebbeim* and teachers, or email me at rabbiyfeigenbaum@gmail.com, and I will try to clarify things.
APPENDIX 12
HALACHOS

This is a very simplified overview of the most common mistakes people make, and a very basic “Uh-oh, what do I do now?” guide. This will not cover all possibilities, and of course in certain areas, there are multiple opinions. Preferably, you should consult your *posek* for detailed guidance of what to do when such issues arise.
If you did not yet say Hashem’s name in the *brachah* of מגן אברהם, go back and say זכרנו לחיים and continue from there. If you already said Hashem’s name, do not go back.
If you did not yet say Hashem’s name in the *brachah* of מחיה המתים, go back and say מי כמוך and continue from there. If you already said Hashem’s name, do not go back.

If you made a mistake and said הקדוש הא-ל, then it depends: If you remembered immediately (the time it takes to say the phrase “שלום עליך רבי”), then quickly correct yourself and say המלך הקדוש. But if more time has passed, you must start your *Shemoneh Esrei* over again from the beginning. If you are not sure what you said, we assume you said what is normally said during the year, and thus you must go back and start *Shemoneh Esrei* over again.
If you did not yet say Hashem’s name in the *brachah* of הטוב שמך, go back and say וכתוב לחיים טובים and continue from there. If you already said Hashem’s name, do not go back.
If you did not yet say Hashem’s name in the *brachah* of המברך את עמו ישראל, go back and say בספר חיים and continue from there. If you already said Hashem’s name, do not go back.

segments.heText:
מַשִׁיב הָרוּחַ
Aוּמוֹרִיד הַגֶּשֶׁם
Aמוֹרִיד הַטָּל

segments.enText: **In the winter**: It depends. If you remembered that you skipped משיב הרוח before you said ונאמן, say it then. If you have already said ונאמן but not yet said the name of Hashem in the *brachah* of מחיה המתים, say משיב הרוח and start ונאמן again. If you have already said the name of Hashem, finish the *brachah* and immediately say משיב הרוח. Even if you finished the *brachah*, if you have not yet said the words אתה קדוש of the next *brachah*, say משיב הרוח immediately and continue. But if you did not remember until after you started אתה קדוש, you must start *Shemoneh Esrei* over again.

But—if you were accustomed to saying מוריד הטל all summer (as does *Nusach Sephard*, as well as *Nusach Ashkenaz* in Israel), then if you forgot to say משיב הרוח during the winter, you do not need to go back, since you said מוריד הטל.

**In the summer**: If by accident you said משיב הרוח—if it is before the *brachah*, then go back and start מכלכל חיים again. If you already said the *brachah*, you must start *Shemoneh Esrei* over again.

**If you are not sure what you said:** Within thirty days of when Pesach began, we assume you said what you had been saying all winter, and within thirty days of Shemini Atzeres we assume you said whatever you had been saying all summer!

segments.heText:
וְתֵן טַל וּמָטָר/
Aוְתֵן טַל וּמָטָר
Aלִבְרָכָה

segments.enText: **In the winter**: It depends. If you remembered that you forgot to say ותן טל ומטר before you said the name of Hashem in the *brachah* of מברך השנים, say ותן טל ומטר then and continue. If you have already said the name of Hashem, add ותן טל ומטר in the *brachah* of שמע קולנו. If you already said רצה, then you must go back to ברך עלינו. If you already have finished *Shemoneh Esrei* (by saying the words יהיו לרצון אמרי פי), you must start *Shemoneh Esrei* over again.

**In the summer:** If you said ומטר instead of טל ברכה, you must go back to ברך עלינו. If you have already finished *Shemoneh Esrei* (by saying the words יהיו לרצון אמרי פי), you must start *Shemoneh Esrei* over again.
**If you are not sure what you said:** Within thirty days of when the change was supposed to take place, we assume you said what you were saying before that.

If you made a mistake and did not make this change to the *brachah*, then it depends: If you remembered immediately (the time it takes to say the phrase “שלום עליך רבי”), then quickly correct yourself and say המלך המשפט. But if more time has passed, then just continue; you do not have to go back to fix this.
**On Rosh Chodesh, *Maariv***: If you forgot to say יעלה ויבא, you do not need to go back.

**On Rosh Chodesh, *Shacharis* and *Minchah***: If you forgot to say יעלה ויבא, then it depends: If you remembered before saying the name of Hashem in the *brachah* of המחזיר שכינתו לציון, then go back to יעלה ויבא. If you finished the *brachah* but did not yet say מודים, then say it there and continue with מודים. If you started מודים, then you must go back to רצה, and if you have already finished *Shemoneh Esrei* (by saying the words יהיו לרצון אמרי פי), you must start *Shemoneh Esrei* over again.
**On Chol Hamoed**: All three *tefillos* have the same laws as Rosh Chodesh *Shacharis* above.
If you are not sure if you said יעלה ויבא, then we assume you did not!

If you forgot to say על הנסים but remembered before you said the name of Hashem in the *brachah* of ולך נאה להודות, then go back to על הנסים and continue from there. But if you already said the name of Hashem in the *brachah* of ולך נאה להודות, do not go back.

If you forgot to say על הנסים but remembered before you said the name of Hashem in the *brachah* of על הארץ ועל המזון, go back to על הנסים and continue from there. But if you already said the name of Hashem in the *brachah* of על הארץ ועל המזון, do not go back.
If you forgot to say רצה at the first two Shabbos meals, you must start over.
**On Rosh Chodesh**: If you forgot to say יעלה ויבא, you do not need to go back.
**On Yom Tov**: If you forgot to say יעלה ויבא at the two Yom Tov meals, you must start over.
**On Chol Hamoed**: If you forgot to say יעלה ויבא, you do not need to go back.
If you are not sure if you said יעלה ויבא, then we assume you did not!

There are many different customs in different communities as to when Tachanun is omitted during *Shacharis* and/or *Minchah*. Here we will present the most basic guidelines of when *Tachanun* is not said, but one should follow the custom of their shul and ask their *rav* for personal guidance in this area.

• After sunset
• In the house of an *Avel*
• If a *chassan* is in shul, the entire week of his *sheva brachos* (There are different opinions regarding the wedding day itself.)
• In a shul where a *bris* is being held that day
• If one of the *baalei ha’bris* (father of the baby, *mohel*, *sandek*) is in shul
• Erev Rosh Hashanah
• Erev Yom Kippur
*Tachanun* is not recited on the following days nor is it recited at *Minchah* preceding these days:

• Shabbos
• Yom Tov
• Chol Hamoed
• Rosh Chodesh
• From Yom Kippur through the day after Sukkos (or till the end of the entire month of Tishrei)
• Chanukah
• Tu B’Shevat
• Purim and Shushan Purim
• Purim Katan (both days)
• The entire month of Nisan
• (Pesach Sheini)
• Lag B’Omer
• From Rosh Chodesh Sivan through the day after Shavuos (or for six days after Shavuos)
• (Tu B’Av)
• Tishah B’Av
